frozen_components: pretrained language models (GPT-2-small, Pythia-70M-deduped, Gemma-2-2B, Llama-3.1-8B)
architecture.summary: A pre-intervention screening framework that predicts sparse autoencoder (SAE) feature steering side effects using intervention-free feature statistics. The system computes predictors from SAE decoder geometry, activation statistics, co-activation structure, and direct-logit footprint before steering, fits ridge regression models to forecast two dimensions of steering modularity (effect stability and collateral spread), and evaluates whether ranking features by predicted cleanliness selects features that steer more reliably on held-out contexts. The mechanism relies on a local-linear approximation of how steering perturbations propagate downstream through the model.
trained_components: ridge regression models predicting steering stability from pre-intervention feature statistics
ridge regression models predicting collateral spread from pre-intervention feature statistics

biblio.title: Pre-Intervention Prediction of Sparse Autoencoder Steering Side Effects

claims.statement: The no-magnitude predictor set improved cross-validated Spearman correlation over the frequency baseline by 0.14 in GPT-2-small, 0.16 in Pythia-70M, 0.08 in Gemma-2-2B, and 0.18 in Llama-3.1-8B, and over the activation-magnitude baseline by 0.10, 0.11, 0.07, and 0.13 respectively.

On downstream-count collateral, decoder crowding (top-k mean absolute cosine) reached univariate Spearman correlation of 0.35 in GPT-2-small, direct-logit footprint reached 0.32 in Pythia-70M, encoder norm reached 0.21 in Gemma-2-2B, and direct-logit footprint reached 0.34 in Llama-3.1-8B.

In GPT-2-small held-out screening on geometry-only selector (32 features per group), predicted-clean features were more stable than predicted-messy features (p=0.012), lower in collateral per effect (p=0.038), and did not differ significantly in effect magnitude (p=0.26).

In Pythia-70M held-out screening, the full no-magnitude selector produced a significant stability contrast (p=0.005, Δμ=0.061) on fresh contexts, while the collateral-per-effect contrast was not significant (p=0.50).

In Llama-3.1-8B held-out screening on the full no-magnitude selector (25 features per group), predicted-clean features had significantly lower collateral per effect (0.064 vs. 0.081, p=0.045) with no significant effect-magnitude difference (p=0.16), while the stability contrast was not significant (p=0.10).

Decoder geometry predictors alone did not transfer consistently: decoder crowding led in GPT-2-small but was weak (Spearman 0.06) for collateral prediction in Pythia-70M, and the dominant predictor signature differed across all four model/SAE settings (GPT-2: decoder crowding; Pythia collateral: direct-logit; Pythia stability: decoder crowding; Gemma: encoder norm; Llama: direct-logit or decoder norm depending on target).

Gemma-2-2B showed weaker predictive transfer: the no-magnitude predictor set improved only 0.08 over frequency (versus 0.14–0.18 in other models), absolute-cosine stability was not predictable by any predictor set (no-magnitude Spearman near zero or negative), and the residualized-stability signal did not survive robustness checks.

Pre-intervention predictability of steering side effects persists under a 4× dictionary-width increase from 32K to 128K Llama Scope SAEs, although the held-out screening payoff becomes less stable and shifts toward a direct-logit stability-only effect, indicating that method-level transfer is more robust than mechanism-level transfer of specific predictor signatures or modularity axes across dictionary settings.
